mappings.rationaleAI: Crustaceans are directly named under 313.F.14.a as a subgroup of Arthropods, covering ~67,000 species with full taxonomic and morphological characterization of the subphylum Crustacea including decapods, copepods, barnacles, and krill.

The article addresses the full span of life's classification — Archaea, Bacteria, Eukaryota (animals, plants, fungi) and viruses — directly covering all major kingdom-level groups enumerated in the section outline, as well as the prokaryote/eukaryote distinction foundational to 313.B and 313.C.

The Animal article covers the entire kingdom Animalia as a classification unit, including major clades (Porifera, Cnidaria, Bilateria — protostomes and deuterostomes) and over 1.5 million described species, directly matching 313.F and its phylum-level subdivisions.

Seeds are the defining reproductive character distinguishing the spermatophytes (seed plants: conifers, ginkgos, cycads, gnetophytes, and angiosperms) enumerated at 313.E.6–E.10, with the article explicitly contrasting gymnosperm and angiosperm seed enclosure.

Biology as a discipline encompasses systematics and the classification of all living things — bacteria, archaea, protists, fungi, plants, and animals — making it a broad but genuine fit for the entire section, particularly through its coverage of cell theory and the diversity of life.

Mollusca maps directly to 313.F.15, with the article covering all major molluscan classes — gastropods, bivalves, cephalopods, scaphopods, and polyplacophorans — precisely as enumerated in the outline's subpaths F.15.a through F.15.h.

Flowers are the defining reproductive structure of angiosperms (magnoliophytes) at 313.E.10, with the article explaining how floral morphology is taxonomically diagnostic and how angiosperms' 150–190-million-year evolution underlies their terrestrial dominance.

The Plant article covers the entire kingdom Plantae from green algae through mosses, ferns, gymnosperms, and angiosperms, matching the full breadth of 313.E including all major subdivisions from bryophytes (E.1) to flowering plants (E.10).

Eukaryotes encompass all the non-prokaryotic kingdoms in the section — protists (C), fungi (D), plants (E), and animals (F) — with the article covering the cell-level distinction from prokaryotes (313.B) and the endosymbiotic origins of plastids relevant to algae/plant classification at 313.C.1 and 313.E.

The Fungus article directly covers 313.D — the kingdom Fungi — with taxonomic detail across seven phyla (Ascomycota, Basidiomycota, etc.), distinguishing fungi from plants, animals, and protists as required by the section.

Fruits are the seed-bearing structures exclusive to angiosperms (313.E.10), and the article's botanical treatment of fruit types (simple, aggregate, multiple) is taxonomically diagnostic for angiosperm classification and distinguishes gymnosperms from angiosperms.

Algae maps directly to the priority path 313.C.1, covering the polyphyletic assemblage of photosynthetic protists — microalgae, cyanobacteria, and macroalgae — including both prokaryotic and eukaryotic lineages that constitute this group in the outline.

The organism concept underlies the entire classification enterprise of Section 313, and the article's treatment of boundary cases (viruses, colonial organisms, lichens) directly addresses classification challenges at 313.A (viruses) and 313.C (protists/slime molds).

Zoology is the scientific discipline dedicated to the structure, classification, and phylogeny of all animals — precisely the content of 313.F — with the article tracing taxonomic frameworks from Aristotle through Linnaeus and molecular systematics.

Insects (class Insecta) are named as part of the Uniramians at 313.F.14.b, with the article covering taxonomic definition, morphological characters, and the over one million described species constituting the most diverse animal group.

Birds (class Aves) are chordates nested under 313.F.25, with the article covering taxonomic placement as theropod dinosaurs, diversity across 44 orders, and phylogenetic relationships — all central to the classification framework of the outline.

Botany is the scientific discipline for the classification and study of plants, covering the ~410,000 land plant species corresponding to the full content of 313.E, and tracing the taxonomic frameworks (Linnaeus's binomial nomenclature, molecular systematics) used to classify plant groups in the outline.

Arthropoda is listed directly at 313.F.14, and the article covers all four subtaxa named in the outline — Chelicerata (F.14.c), Crustacea (F.14.a), and Hexapoda/Uniramia (F.14.b) — with full taxonomic and morphological characterization.
